items.prompt: When do crowdsourcing advantages matter most for annotation?
choices: When the schema is simple, guidelines are clear, and gold checks are in place
When every item requires board-certified clinical judgment
When no quality control is allowed
When labels must be produced by a single expert only
items.explain: Section 11.9.1 ties crowd advantages to simple schemas plus Chapter 4 gold checks.

items.prompt: Example 11.16 uses crowds to finish millions of attribute tags in days rather than months.
items.explain: Crowd scale distributes the schema across many workers for high-volume face attributes.

items.prompt: What does Example 11.20 illustrate about radiology labels?
choices: Board-certified radiologists produce higher-precision tumor masks than general crowd workers
Crowd workers outperform radiologists on tumor boundaries
No humans should label MRI slices
Legal experts should label all medical images
items.explain: Expert clinical training yields higher-precision masks than non-expert crowd workers.

items.prompt: Name two primary challenges of expert-only annotation.
items.rubric: Cost, limited scalability/availability, or slower throughput.
items.sample_answer: High cost and limited scalability because skilled experts are scarce and slow.

items.prompt: Example 11.22 argues that non-experts routinely misread operative contract clauses.
items.explain: Legal experts identify operative clauses and defined terms that non-experts misread.
